text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So, I mean, obviously, as is the case with a lot of these trends, you know, on the Apple iPhone side, the new iPhones, I actually don't have the latest iPhone, by the way, but the newest iPhones now come that you don't have a SIM card tray.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's all eSIMs.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: eSIMs, I mean, folks can Google this, but it's like this technology where you don't actually have to put a physical card in your phones anymore.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You can pretty much just use like a specific code.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You send your IMEI code and, you know, a bunch of the other things.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: and your telecom provider can basically just send you an eSIM that you can download onto your phone, and it basically replaces your SIM card, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And the reason I wanted to have more than one eSIM is because, like I said, when I traveled to India, I wanted to use the same phone and have two SIMs active.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And most phones can do this, by the way.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So like, you know...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: All the Android phones, the most recent Android phones and the iPhones, they all allow you to have two SIM cards.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The problem with the Pixel 7, though, is in order to have two active lines, one of them has to be a physical SIM and the other one is like an eSIM.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The iPhones, however, allow you to have both of them as eSIMs.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And for me, the problem is I have...

text: That's a pain.

text: And interestingly, you and I had a chat that I thought would be interesting to our listeners around being able to ship things in time.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So yeah, help set the context for this, because I thought this was really interesting and something I feel all developers go through at some point.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, for sure.

text: Um, so background for anyone who's new is listening.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Uh, I've been developing software for over 20 years now.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Dang.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you would think that I have this down to a science and for some things, maybe I do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Uh, but there's still a lot of times where I might be working at a client or a company or whatever, and I might need to make a change to code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And in that when I'm changing something, uh,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I might have to dig deep into the code half the day to figure out what it's even doing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I might make 10, 20, 30 changes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I might change 30 lines of code in an entire day.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then sometimes I'll look back and be like, what am I doing?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Am I even a real programmer anymore?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm barely modifying this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this is what I'm shipping as my productive day.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I get down on myself about it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's been to the point a few times where I look back and wonder, I'm like, the real question is, am I a programmer?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Can I even really build things anymore?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I've built a lot of things.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's like, well...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's been a while since I've kind of built something from the ground up myself.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what I wanted to do was just kind of give myself a little test and say, hey, how can I verify that I can still ship something in a timely manner?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And recently, what I decided to do a few weeks ago was just sit down and create a product within 24 hours from a concept to shipping it live.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it was a web product, so it was a web app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It allowed me to actually realize like, oh, Don, you still know how to build things.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You still know how to ship.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You still know how to prioritize.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it gave me at the end of it, it gave me a lot of confidence because I realized I can still do all these things.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It just depends upon the context in which I'm working determines the outcome of what I'm doing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if it's something that's super old legacy code, doesn't have any tests, hard to decipher.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Yeah, it's like walking through really thick mud that's really hard to get through and it's hard to be productive.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But if I'm building something brand new with a technology that I know, like the back of my hand, I can be a sprinter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I can just really fly and build and that helps me build the confidence and helps me build the muscle that, hey, I still have this and I can still do what I think.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I thought I could do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so it helps clear that, like that fear of, can I do it?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The uncertainty of, am I still a real programmer?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And, you know, helps kind of curb some of the doubt of what I'm doing on a day-to-day basis.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I feel like that's something, as someone with 20 plus years of experience still fighting, if I'm dealing with it, I imagine someone else is probably still dealing with it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So as I was saying, that's kind of what I recently did.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it was almost very cathartic in a sense to kind of just...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They need help with some architecture or whatever, but
